title: Probability & Counting
blurb: Counting principles, permutations and combinations, and probability of compound events.

lessons.title: The Counting Principle
lessons.concreteComparison: You own 3 shirts and 2 pairs of pants. How many outfits? Not $3 + 2 = 5$ — each shirt pairs with each pant: $3 \times 2 = 6$. That little ×, applied stage by stage, counts phone numbers, passwords, license plates, and DNA — without ever listing them. It's the engine under everything this unit does.
lessons.objective: Count multi-stage choices with the multiplication principle, with and without repetition allowed.
concept: **The counting principle**: if a task has stages with $m$, then $n$, then $p$ options, the total is $m \times n \times p$. Outfits: $3 \times 2 = 6$; a menu of 4 mains, 3 drinks, 2 desserts: $4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 = 24$ dinners.
Why multiply: each of the 3 shirts opens 2 pant-doors — 3 groups of 2. A tree diagram of the choices has exactly $3 \times 2$ leaves.
**Repetition allowed** keeps every stage full: a 4-digit PIN has $10 \cdot 10 \cdot 10 \cdot 10 = 10^4 = 10000$ options.
**No repetition** shrinks each stage: a 4-digit code with all digits different has $10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 \cdot 7 = 5040$. The next lesson gives that shrinking pattern a name.
lessons.keyIdea: Stages multiply: m × n × p total outcomes. Repetition keeps stages full; no-repetition shrinks them by one each time.

workedExamples.statement: A cafe offers 4 mains, 3 drinks, and 2 desserts. How many different meals?
How many 4-digit PINs are possible? How many with no repeated digit?
License plates: 2 letters then 3 digits. How many plates?

commonMistakes.text: Adding the stages: 3 shirts + 2 pants = 5 outfits.
commonMistakes.correction: Each shirt goes with EACH pant — 3 groups of 2, so $3 \times 2 = 6$. Adding counts the clothes, not the outfits.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting stages shrink when repetition is banned.
commonMistakes.correction: "4 digits, all different" is $10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 \cdot 7$, not $10^4$ — each pick uses up an option.

tryIt.statement: 5 sandwich breads, 4 fillings: how many sandwiches?
3-character codes from A–Z, no repeats: how many?

steps.title: The outfit problem
beats: 3 shirts, 2 pants. List the outfits? S1P1, S1P2, S2P1…
Multiply the stages. Never list again.

steps.title: Bigger menus
beats: Any number of stages: keep multiplying.

steps.title: Multiply, don't add
steps.prompt: A quiz has 5 true/false questions. How many answer sheets are possible?

steps.title: Full vs shrinking stages
beats: All digits DIFFERENT: first pick 10 ways…
Read the fine print: may options repeat?

steps.title: Repeats or not?
steps.prompt: How many 3-letter codes use three DIFFERENT letters?
steps.explanation: No repeats: stages shrink $26, 25, 24$ → $15600$. ($26^3 = 17576$ allows AAA.)

steps.title: Stage engineering
steps.intro: Identify the stages; count each; multiply.

examples.prompt: A restaurant's 'combo': 3 soups, 5 mains, 4 desserts, 2 drinks. Total combos?
How many 5-digit numbers exist (first digit can't be 0)?
Password: exactly 2 letters then 2 digits, no repeated characters. Count them.

problems.prompt: 4 routes from home to school, 3 from school to the gym. Routes home→gym?
A 6-question multiple-choice quiz, 4 options each: possible answer sheets?
problems.explanation: Six stages of 4: $4^6$. (Base = options, exponent = questions.)

problems.prompt: 2-digit codes with different digits: how many?

steps.title: Why your padlock is called a 'combination' lock (wrongly)
steps.body: A 3-dial lock with digits 0–9 has $10^3 = 1000$ settings — countable in an afternoon by a patient thief, which is why bike locks got 4 and 5 dials ($10000$ and $100000$). And mathematically it's a PERMUTATION lock: 1-2-3 and 3-2-1 open different doors. The right name was inside this unit all along.

lessons.title: Permutations
lessons.concreteComparison: Eight sprinters, three medals. Gold has 8 candidates, silver 7, bronze 6: $8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6 = 336$ podiums. That shrinking product has a name — a **permutation** — and a notation built on the **factorial**, math's fastest-growing everyday function.
lessons.objective: Count ordered arrangements with factorials and the permutation formula $_nP_r = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!}$.
concept: **Factorial**: $n! = n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots 1$ counts ways to arrange ALL of $n$ things in order. $5! = 120$ ways to shelve 5 books. (And $0! = 1$: one way to arrange nothing.)
Arranging just $r$ of the $n$: the stages shrink from $n$ but stop after $r$ picks — that's a **permutation**: $_nP_r = n(n-1)\cdots$ ($r$ factors) $= \frac{n!}{(n-r)!}$.
Podium: $_8P_3 = 8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6 = 336 = \frac{8!}{5!}$ — the $5!$ cancels the un-used tail.
**Order matters** is the permutation signature: gold-silver-bronze, president-then-VP, 1st-2nd-3rd. If swapping two picks changes the outcome, you're permuting.
lessons.keyIdea: n! arranges everything; nPr = n!/(n−r)! arranges r of n. Use when ORDER MATTERS.

facts.title: Permutation
facts.explanation: r picks from n, order mattering.

workedExamples.statement: How many ways can 5 books be arranged on a shelf?
A club of 10 picks a president, then a vice-president. How many outcomes?
workedExamples.solution: $_{10}P_2 = 10 \cdot 9 = 90$ — order matters: the two jobs differ.

commonMistakes.correction: $_8P_3$ is $8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6$ — the SHRINKING product with $r$ factors, not $8 \cdot 3 = 24$ or $8^3 = 512$.
$0! = 1$: there is exactly ONE way to arrange nothing (do nothing). The formulas depend on it: $_nP_n = \tfrac{n!}{0!} = n!$ ✓.

steps.title: The factorial
beats: 5 books on a shelf: 5 choices, then 4, 3, 2, 1.

steps.title: Enter the permutation
beats: Formula: $\tfrac{8!}{5!}$ — divide away the unused tail.

steps.title: Order matters
beats: Gold-silver-bronze: swapping runners CHANGES the podium.
President then VP: swapping changes who leads.
(Order NOT mattering is next lesson's story.)

steps.title: Which is a permutation?
steps.prompt: Which task needs a permutation count?
steps.explanation: Prizes ranked = order matters. Equal committee members and identical prizes don't care about order — different tool.

steps.title: Ordered counting
steps.intro: Factorial for all; nPr for some.

examples.prompt: How many ways can 6 students line up for a photo?
A playlist shows 4 of your 9 favorite songs, in order. How many playlists?
In how many ways can the letters of MATH be arranged?

problems.prompt: 12 teams, top-3 trophy finishes: how many outcomes?

problems.explanation: Arranging all of them IS the factorial: $\tfrac{n!}{0!} = n!$ (thanks to $0! = 1$).

steps.title: 52! — the number that breaks intuition
steps.body: Shuffle a deck properly and its order is one of $52! \approx 8 \times 10^{67}$ arrangements — so many that every fair shuffle in history has almost certainly produced an order NO ONE has ever seen. There are more deck orders than atoms in our galaxy. You've held a cosmic first in your hands and never noticed.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Use permutations when swapping picks changes the outcome.

lessons.title: Combinations
lessons.concreteComparison: Pick 2 of 5 friends for pizza — does "Ana then Bat" differ from "Bat then Ana"? No: same pizza table. Permutations would count both ($5 \cdot 4 = 20$); reality counts them once ($10$). When order does NOT matter, divide out the repeats: that's a **combination**, the workhorse of teams, hands, and lotteries.
lessons.objective: Count unordered selections with $_nC_r = \frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!}$, and choose correctly between permutations and combinations.
concept: **Combination**: choose $r$ of $n$ with order irrelevant. Formula: $_nC_r = \frac{n!}{r!\,(n-r)!}$ — the permutation count divided by $r!$, the number of orders each group was over-counted in.
Pizza pair: $_5C_2 = \tfrac{5 \cdot 4}{2!} = 10$. Each pair {Ana, Bat} appeared as 2 orderings — dividing by $2! = 2$ collapses them.
The decision test: **does swapping change anything?** President/VP — yes: permutation. Two equal committee seats — no: combination. Medals — permutation. Pizza toppings — combination.
Symmetry bonus: $_nC_r = {_nC_{n-r}}$ — choosing 2 to take IS choosing 3 to leave: $_5C_2 = {_5C_3} = 10$.
lessons.keyIdea: nCr = n! / (r!(n−r)!): permutations with the r! orderings divided out. Use when order doesn't matter.

facts.title: Symmetry
facts.explanation: Choosing the keepers = choosing the leftovers.

workedExamples.statement: How many ways to choose 2 of 5 friends?
A team of 4 from a class of 10: how many teams?
From 8 candidates: (a) president + VP, (b) two equal reps. Count each.
workedExamples.solution: (a) order matters: $8 \cdot 7 = 56$. (b) order doesn't: $_8C_2 = 28$ — exactly half.

commonMistakes.text: Using a permutation for an order-blind choice.
commonMistakes.correction: Two equal committee seats from 8 people is $_8C_2 = 28$, not $56$ — Ana-with-Bat IS Bat-with-Ana. Ask the swap test first.
Choosing 3 of 7: $\tfrac{7 \cdot 6 \cdot 5}{3!} = \tfrac{210}{6} = 35$. Each trio was counted in $3! = 6$ orders — divide by all six.

tryIt.statement: Choose 3 pizza toppings from 9 available: how many ways?

steps.title: Same pizza, two names
beats: But 'Ana then Bat' = 'Bat then Ana'. Same table!

steps.title: Collapse the orders
steps.explanation: $\tfrac{7 \cdot 6}{2!} = 21$. ($42$ forgot to divide; $14$ divided by $r$ instead of $r!$… same here, but wrong habit.)

steps.title: Permutation or combination?
beats: Swapping two picks changes the outcome? PERMUTATION.
Two equal reps: swap → same pair. C.
Medals: P. Toppings: C. Always run the test.

steps.title: Which needs a combination?
steps.explanation: Lottery numbers win as a SET — order irrelevant: $_nC_5$. Medals and rankings change on swap: permutations.

steps.title: Group counting
steps.intro: Swap test first; then the right formula.

examples.prompt: A 5-player starting lineup (no positions) from 12 players?
How many different 2-topping pizzas from 8 toppings?
steps: Toppings mix in a pan — order-blind.

problems.prompt: Choose 3 books to pack (from 10): how many ways?
problems.explanation: A bag doesn't care about order: $_{10}C_3 = 120$. ($720$ is the permutation.)

problems.prompt: 4 equal-prize raffle winners from 20 tickets: combination or permutation?
problems.explanation: Equal prizes: swapping winners changes nothing. $_{20}C_4 = 4845$.

steps.title: Pascal's triangle is a combination table
steps.body: Stack the numbers $_nC_r$ into rows and you get Pascal's triangle — each entry the sum of the two above it. The same triangle expands $(a+b)^n$, counts lattice paths, and hides the Fibonacci numbers along its diagonals. Blaise Pascal built it in 1654… to settle a gambling dispute. Combinations were born at a casino table.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Swap test: outcome changes → P; doesn't → C.

lessons.title: Probability Basics
lessons.concreteComparison: Roll a die. Six faces, each equally likely, and "roll a 5" claims exactly one of them: $P = \tfrac{1}{6}$. That's the whole foundation — **probability = favorable over total** — a number from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain) that quantifies luck itself.
lessons.objective: Compute probabilities as favorable/total for equally likely outcomes, use the 0-to-1 scale, and apply the complement rule.
concept: For equally likely outcomes: $P(A) = \frac{\text{favorable}}{\text{total}}$. One die, 'roll a 5': $\tfrac16$. 'Roll even': three faces → $\tfrac36 = \tfrac12$.

The scale: $0 \le P \le 1$. $P = 0$ impossible ('roll a 7'), $P = 1$ certain ('roll at most 6'), $\tfrac12$ = coin-flip odds. Probabilities are fractions, decimals, or percents — same number, three outfits.
**Complement rule**: $P(\text{not } A) = 1 - P(A)$. Chance a card is NOT a heart: $1 - \tfrac{13}{52} = \tfrac{39}{52} = \tfrac34$. Often the 'not' is the easy side to count.
Count carefully — the classic error is wrong TOTALS: two dice have $36$ ordered outcomes, not $12$ ('sum of 7' is $\tfrac{6}{36}$, and sums aren't equally likely!).
lessons.keyIdea: P = favorable/total (equally likely!), lives in [0,1]; P(not A) = 1 − P(A).

workedExamples.statement: A standard deck (52 cards): $P(\text{heart})$? $P(\text{not a heart})$?
A bag holds 3 red, 5 blue, 2 green marbles. $P(\text{blue})$? $P(\text{not green})$?

commonMistakes.correction: 'Roll a 5' is $\tfrac{1}{6}$ (one of six faces), not $\tfrac{1}{5}$ (one against five). The denominator is ALL outcomes.

commonMistakes.text: Applying favorable/total to outcomes that are NOT equally likely.
commonMistakes.correction: Two-dice sums run 2–12, but $P(\text{sum}=7) \neq \tfrac{1}{11}$: sums aren't equally likely. Count the 36 ordered PAIRS: $\tfrac{6}{36} = \tfrac16$.

tryIt.statement: A spinner has 8 equal sectors, 3 of them gold. $P(\text{gold})$?

steps.title: Favorable over total
beats: A die: 6 faces, all equally likely.
Count both floors. Divide. That's probability.

steps.title: Count both floors
steps.explanation: Favorable 4, TOTAL 10 (not 6 — that's favorable-over-unfavorable, the odds trap).

steps.title: Count the easy side
beats: Sometimes 'not' is MUCH easier to count…
…a trick that pays off big next lesson.

steps.title: Complement
steps.prompt: A spinner lands on red with $P = \tfrac{2}{7}$. $P(\text{not red})$ = …

examples.prompt: One card from a full deck: $P(\text{face card})$? (J, Q, K in 4 suits)
A class: 12 girls, 8 boys. A random student is picked. $P(\text{boy})$, as a percent?

problems.prompt: A letter is picked from MONGOL. $P(\text{O})$ = …

problems.explanation: Probabilities live in $[0, 1]$ — $1.2$ would be 'more than certain'.

steps.title: The problem that started it all
steps.body: Probability theory began in 1654 when a gambler asked Pascal and Fermat how to fairly split the pot of an interrupted dice game. Their letters back and forth — counting favorable outcomes over total outcomes — founded the field that now prices insurance, trains AI models, and forecasts elections. All of it: favorable over total.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $P(\text{not }A) = 1 - P(A)$: count whichever side is easier.

lessons.title: Compound Events
lessons.concreteComparison: One coin lands heads half the time. Two coins BOTH heads? Half of the half: $\tfrac12 \cdot \tfrac12 = \tfrac14$. Chains of events multiply their probabilities — as long as they don't influence each other. And when one draw changes the next (a card kept out of the deck), the fractions update mid-chain. Two rules, all of compound probability.
lessons.objective: Multiply probabilities of independent events, adjust for dependence (without replacement), and use the complement for 'at least one'.
concept: **Independent** events (neither affects the other) multiply: $P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. Two dice, both sixes: $\tfrac16 \cdot \tfrac16 = \tfrac{1}{36}$.
**Dependent** events update the second fraction: bag of 5 red + 3 blue, draw two WITHOUT replacement, both red: $\tfrac58 \cdot \tfrac47 = \tfrac{5}{14}$ — after one red leaves, 4 reds remain of 7.

**At least one** = complement of NONE: $P(\text{at least one six in two rolls}) = 1 - (\tfrac56)^2 = \tfrac{11}{36}$. Counting 'none' is one clean multiplication; counting 'at least one' directly is a mess.
Watch the words: WITH replacement → fractions reset (independent); WITHOUT → they shift. And 'or' with no overlap ADDS: $P(\text{heart or spade}) = \tfrac14 + \tfrac14 = \tfrac12$.
lessons.keyIdea: Independent: multiply. Without replacement: update the fractions. 'At least one': 1 − P(none).

facts.title: At least one
facts.explanation: The complement does the hard case.

workedExamples.statement: Bag: 5 red, 3 blue. Two draws, no replacement: $P(\text{both red})$?

commonMistakes.correction: 'And' chains MULTIPLY: $\tfrac16 \cdot \tfrac16 = \tfrac{1}{36}$. (Adding is for non-overlapping 'or'.) Both sixes is RARER than one six, not more likely.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to update fractions without replacement.
commonMistakes.correction: Both red from 5R/3B: $\tfrac58 \cdot \tfrac58$ is WITH replacement. Without: the second draw sees 4 reds among 7 — $\tfrac58 \cdot \tfrac47$.

tryIt.statement: A coin and a die: $P(\text{heads AND a six})$?
Three coin flips: $P(\text{at least one head})$?

steps.title: Half of a half
beats: Two coins BOTH heads: half of the times the first is…
$\tfrac12 \cdot \tfrac12 = \tfrac14$. Check the list: HH, HT, TH, TT ✓.

steps.title: Without replacement
beats: Bag: 5 red, 3 blue. Draw two, keep the first out.
First red: $\tfrac58$. Now the bag CHANGED: 4 red of 7.

steps.title: Second fraction
steps.prompt: Bag: 4 white, 2 black; two draws, no replacement. $P(\text{both white})$ = …

steps.title: Attack the complement
beats: 'At least one six in two rolls' — many cases! (first, second, both…)
See 'at least one'? Compute NONE, subtract from 1.

examples.prompt: A quiz has two multiple-choice questions (4 options each). $P(\text{guess both right})$?
Deck of 52, draw two without replacement: $P(\text{both aces})$?
Free-throw shooter makes 80%. $P(\text{at least one make in two shots})$?

steps.title: Chain-craft
steps.intro: Independent multiply; dependent update; 'at least' flips.

problems.prompt: Two spins of a 5-sector spinner (1 gold sector): $P(\text{gold twice})$ = …
Drawer: 6 black socks, 4 white. Grab two (no replacement): $P(\text{both black})$ = …

steps.title: The gambler's fallacy
steps.body: A roulette wheel in Monte Carlo, 1913: black came up 26 times straight, and gamblers lost fortunes betting red 'because it was due'. It wasn't — each spin is INDEPENDENT, and the wheel has no memory. $P(\text{red})$ stayed the same on spin 27 as on spin 1. Multiplication of independent events isn't just homework; it's the fallacy-proof vest.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Without replacement: update the second fraction.
'At least one' = $1 - P(\text{none})$ — the complement shortcut.

lessons.title: Probability Meets Counting
lessons.concreteComparison: A mini-lottery: pick 3 numbers from 10; the draw picks 3. What's your chance? Favorable: 1 (your exact set). Total: $_{10}C_3 = 120$ equally likely sets. $P = \tfrac{1}{120}$. When outcomes get too many to list, the counting tools from lessons 1–3 BECOME the probability tools — this is where the unit's two halves shake hands.
lessons.objective: Compute probabilities whose favorable and total counts come from the counting principle, permutations, and combinations.
concept: The framework is unchanged — $P = \tfrac{\text{favorable}}{\text{total}}$ — but both counts now come from formulas: totals from $_nC_r$, $_nP_r$, or stage products; favorables the same way.
Mini-lottery: total $_{10}C_3 = 120$; favorable 1 → $P = \tfrac{1}{120}$. Real lotteries just use bigger numbers: 6-from-45 gives $_{45}C_6 = 8{,}145{,}060$.
Richer favorables count too: committee of 2 from 4 girls + 3 boys; $P(\text{both girls}) = \tfrac{_4C_2}{_7C_2} = \tfrac{6}{21} = \tfrac27$.
Cross-check trick: many counting probabilities also fall to the chain rule. Both girls: $\tfrac47 \cdot \tfrac36 = \tfrac27$ ✓. Two roads, one answer — use one to verify the other.
lessons.keyIdea: P = favorable/total with both counts from counting formulas — and the chain rule cross-checks the answer.

workedExamples.statement: From 4 girls and 3 boys, a random pair is chosen. $P(\text{both girls})$?
Four people sit in a random row. $P(\text{Ana and Bat sit together})$?
workedExamples.solution: Total $4! = 24$; together: treat the pair as one block — $3! \cdot 2 = 12$: $P = \tfrac{12}{24} = \tfrac12$.

commonMistakes.text: Mixing an ordered total with an unordered favorable (or vice versa).
commonMistakes.correction: If the total counts SETS ($_nC_r$), the favorable must count sets too. Half-ordered fractions are the top source of wrong probabilities.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting that only EQUALLY LIKELY totals work.
commonMistakes.correction: The framework needs every counted outcome equally likely — random draws and fair seatings qualify; 'sum of two dice' values don't (count the 36 pairs instead).

tryIt.statement: Choose 2 from 6 red + 2 gold marbles. $P(\text{both gold})$?

steps.title: Scale it up
steps.prompt: A 6-from-45 lottery has $_{45}C_6 = 8145060$ sets. One ticket's jackpot chance is…
steps.explanation: One favorable set of 8.1 million equally likely sets. (You are ~40× likelier to be struck by lightning this year.)

steps.title: Both from one group
steps.prompt: From 5 novels + 3 comics, two random books. $P(\text{both comics})$ = …

steps.title: Two roads, one answer
beats: Solve one way, VERIFY the other. Free error-proofing.

steps.title: The unit, united
steps.intro: Choose the counting tool; run the fraction; cross-check.

examples.prompt: Three people randomly line up. $P(\text{alphabetical order})$?
Committee of 3 from 5 seniors + 4 juniors: $P(\text{all seniors})$?
A 4-digit PIN is chosen at random. $P(\text{all digits different})$?

problems.prompt: 5 friends randomly pick seats in a row of 5. $P(\text{a particular friend gets the left end})$ = …
problems.explanation: Favorable: $4! = 24$ arrangements with her on the end; total $5! = 120$: $\tfrac{24}{120} = \tfrac15$. (Or simply: 5 equally likely seats.)

problems.prompt: A raffle sells 100 tickets; you hold 5. Two different winners are drawn. $P(\text{you win NEITHER})$ = …
problems.explanation: Without replacement: $\tfrac{95}{100} \cdot \tfrac{94}{99} = \tfrac{893}{990}$ — close to $(0.95)^2$ but not equal: the second draw remembers.

steps.title: The birthday surprise
steps.body: In a class of just 23 people, the probability that SOME two share a birthday is over 50% — computed exactly this unit's way: $P(\text{no shared}) = \tfrac{365}{365} \cdot \tfrac{364}{365} \cdots$ (23 shrinking factors) $\approx 0.49$, so $P(\text{shared}) \approx 0.51$. Twenty-three people, half a coin-flip. Counting doesn't care what intuition expects.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Counting formulas feed both floors of $P = \tfrac{\text{fav}}{\text{total}}$.
Keep ordered with ordered, sets with sets.
Cross-check with the chain rule — two roads, one answer.

practice.statement: 6 shirt colors, 3 logo options: how many shirt designs?
How many 3-digit codes (digits may repeat)? With no repeats?
President + secretary from 9 people vs a 2-person team from 9: count both.
Two independent free throws at 70%: $P(\text{both made})$?
Bag: 6 green, 4 yellow; two draws, no replacement. $P(\text{both green})$?
Random pair from 3 teachers + 5 students: $P(\text{both students})$?

testYourself.statement: A menu: 5 mains, 4 sides, 3 drinks. How many meals?
How many ways can 7 books line a shelf?
Gold/silver/bronze among 10 gymnasts: how many podiums?
A 4-person committee from 11 volunteers: how many?
$P(\text{at least one six in three rolls})$?
